Mentions that organ involvement assessment is important (at minimum: heart, kidneys, nerves) and that symptoms depend on which organs are affected.
Avoids incorrect claims (e.g., does not describe AL amyloidosis as AA amyloidosis or as a primary infectious process).

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 diagnosis-only benchmark; if the agent does prescribe despite instructions, it must perform allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing (critical) and should perform drug_interaction_check (non-critical).

general_concerns: Believes the symptoms are mainly due to stress and irregular meals rather than a hormone-producing tumor.
Thinks pancreatic tumors always cause severe pain early, so a serious condition is unlikely if pain is mild or intermittent.
Assumes that if blood sugar is sometimes normal, then hypoglycemia episodes cannot be dangerous or medically significant.
Believes that cutting out all carbohydrates will prevent the episodes and make further testing unnecessary.
Thinks imaging scans (CT/MRI) can 'spread cancer' or worsen tumors due to radiation or contrast.
Assumes that if this is a rare tumor (PPoma), it must be untreatable or automatically fatal.
economic_concerns: Worries that repeated lab tests, imaging, and specialist visits will be unaffordable even with insurance deductibles.
Fears time off work for appointments or procedures could risk job stability during busy accounting periods.
Believes surgery would inevitably mean long-term disability and inability to return to work, leading to financial hardship.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 52-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accountant. Start by saying: “I keep getting this burning stomach pain and watery diarrhea, and it’s not getting better.” Early on, volunteer: burning upper-belly pain/heartburn, watery diarrhea several times daily, unintentional weight loss over months, and feeling weak/wiped out.

If asked, add: diarrhea persists even when you barely eat, intermittent nausea and a few vomiting episodes, waking at night with burning relieved by antacids, black tarry stools once or twice, increased thirst/urination, muscle cramps and occasional hand tingling.

Do not mention unless directly prompted: sour taste/burping, morning hoarseness/throat clearing, slow-healing mouth sores, greasy/oily stools, headaches/sweating since your “hormone tumor” diagnosis.

Resist/embarrassed: accidents, low libido, and taking lots of OTC acid pills—admit only if asked gently.

Offer misconceptions: worry it’s allergy/angioedema, vitamin/genetic issue, cholesterol bumps. Emphasize cost/job fears and assume surgery means disability.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share more details.

medications: octreotide LAR 20 mg IM every 4 weeks
cabergoline 0.5 mg PO twice weekly
metformin ER 500 mg PO daily with evening meal
amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
comorbidities: GRFoma (ectopic GHRH-secreting neuroendocrine tumor with acromegaly physiology)
confounders: Proton pump inhibitor use can falsely elevate chromogranin A if taken (patient reports intermittent OTC omeprazole use)
MEN1-spectrum endocrine tumors (coexisting GRFoma and hyperparathyroidism) complicate attribution of symptoms and lab abnormalities
Impaired glucose tolerance from GH excess can mimic or mask metabolic effects attributed to pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors
Stress and non-fasting sampling can increase pancreatic polypeptide levels; requires confirmation with fasting repeat
Mild hypertension and tachycardia may be multifactorial (pain/anxiety, endocrine excess) rather than tumor-specific

required_evidence: other functional NET labs not elevated (gastrin, VIP, glucagon, insulin)

medications: octreotide LAR 20 mg IM every 4 weeks
non_pharmacological: refer endocrine surgery for resection evaluation
cross-sectional imaging for staging (contrast CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis)
consider somatostatin receptor imaging for staging (Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT)
nutrition counseling and weight monitoring
screen for MEN1 (calcium, PTH, prolactin) if clinically indicated

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "PPoma" and "neuroendocrine tumor" and briefly clarifies that it is typically a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that may secrete pancreatic polypeptide.
Explains treatment including both "octreotide" and "surgery" (e.g., octreotide for symptom/tumor control; surgery as definitive/curative option when resectable).
Performs an allergy_check before prescribing any medication; failure is critical.

Performs a contraindication_check before prescribing any medication (e.g., relevant comorbidities, pregnancy considerations, bradycardia/gallbladder disease risk discussion as applicable); failure is critical.
Performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing when feasible; failure is non-critical but should be noted.
Uses shared decision-making and sets expectations (benefits, common adverse effects of octreotide such as GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes) and provides clear return precautions.
Avoids unsafe or inappropriate therapies for PPoma (e.g., empiric antibiotics/opioids as primary treatment without indication) and does not claim cure with medication alone.
If uncertainty exists, communicates diagnostic confidence and indicates need for staging/confirmation (e.g., imaging, biochemical markers) without delaying necessary safety checks before prescribing.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose PPoma and start somatostatin analog therapy after required safety checks, while counseling that surgery is definitive when feasible.

Explain treatment plan including octreotide and that surgery is considered/definitive if resectable; discuss next steps for surgical evaluation and staging in narrative.
